title: Housing
summary: Find a realistic place to stay and understand rental basics before arrival.
body:
Housing is the most time-sensitive part of settling in. Start early, check whether your university has reserved rooms or vetted partners, and be careful with deposits, identity documents, and contracts.

First-year international students are usually offered housing by the university - either on or off campus - through the Roomspot portal. This is the most straightforward option and worth applying for as early as possible.

Outside of Roomspot, students can browse private rental listings on sites like Pararius, Huurwoningen, Xior, and Plaza. These can be competitive, so start looking early and be cautious with any listing that asks for payment or personal documents before a contract is signed.

Rent benefit (huurtoeslag)
If you are renting independently and your income and rent fall within certain limits, you may be eligible for huurtoeslag - a monthly rent benefit paid by the Dutch government to help cover housing costs. You need a BSN and a DigiD to apply, and the rental property must meet specific requirements such as being a self-contained unit with a registered contract. Not all student rooms qualify, but it is worth checking. You can apply through the Belastingdienst website.

title: Visa and residence
summary: Understand whether you need an MVV, residence permit, biometrics, or permit pickup.
body: Immigration steps depend on nationality and study programme. Your university is often the recognised sponsor for study permits, so follow its deadlines and keep IND messages together.

title: Documents
summary: Prepare identification, admission, housing, insurance, and legalised documents.
body: Documents are easier to fix before departure than after arrival. Make a digital and paper folder and check whether any civil documents need legalisation, apostille, or translation.

title: Municipality, BSN, and DigiD
summary: Register with the municipality, receive your BSN, and set up DigiD.
body:
These three things are connected and need to happen in order. If you're staying in the Netherlands for more than four months, this chain is one of the most important things to sort out in your first weeks.

Municipality registration
When you move to the Netherlands, you need to register your address with the local municipality (gemeente). For UT students, that's the gemeente Enschede. This goes into the BRP (Basisregistratie Personen), which is the Dutch national population register. You register in person at the municipality office - you'll need to book an appointment. Bring your passport, housing contract, and any civil documents they ask for. Note that you need a confirmed address before you can register, which is why housing is the first thing to sort.

BSN
Your BSN (Burgerservicenummer) is your Dutch citizen service number - a unique personal number used to identify you across healthcare, taxes, banking, and government services. You get your BSN at or shortly after your municipality registration appointment. Keep it somewhere safe but accessible - you'll be asked for it often, especially when opening a bank account, registering with a GP, and setting up DigiD.

DigiD
DigiD is your digital identity for the Netherlands - a login that lets you access government websites, healthcare portals, tax services, and some university systems online. You apply for DigiD online after you have your BSN. The process sends an activation letter to your registered address - activate it before the code expires. Make sure you have a Dutch or reliable EU phone number set up before you apply, as DigiD uses two-factor authentication via SMS and your home country number can sometimes cause issues.

The order matters
Housing > Municipality registration > BSN > DigiD > everything else.

title: Money and banking
summary: Plan access to money and arrange payment methods.
body:
Getting banking sorted is one of the first things that actually unblocks everything else - rent, transport, subscriptions, and splitting costs with people.

Your main Dutch bank account
UT recommends ABN AMRO or ING for international students. Both offer a free student package and are used to dealing with internationals.

You should be able to open an account through online banking using your ID and a Dutch phone number. Once you're registered with the municipality, you'll need to upload proof of residence to fully activate the account. If you don't have your BSN yet when you apply, you can add it later.

Make sure to look up the official website of banks before starting the process for the latest information.

While you're waiting: Revolut or Bunq
There's usually a gap between arriving and getting your Dutch bank account fully up and running - especially if your BSN or proof of residence takes time. This is where a lot of students set up a secondary account with Revolut or Bunq. Neither requires a BSN or Dutch address to get started, so you can have payment access from day one.

Tikkie
Once you have a Dutch bank account, you'll almost immediately encounter Tikkie. It's a payment request app that nearly everyone in the Netherlands uses for splitting costs - dinner, groceries, shared household bills, anything.

title: Healthcare and GP
summary: Choose the right insurance and know how to access non-urgent care.
body:
Health insurance
The right insurance for you depends on what you're doing in the Netherlands and where you're from.

If you're a non-EU student studying only (no paid work or internship), you cannot take out Dutch basic health insurance. You need international student insurance instead. The recommended option for UT students is AON Student Insurance. It's designed specifically for international students and covers medical costs including pre-existing conditions, personal liability, and repatriation. You can arrange it online via their website before you arrive.

If you're an EU/EEA student, your European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) covers essential medical care during your stay. However, it may not cover everything - liability, repatriation, and some medical costs may not be included. AON offers supplementary coverage for EU students too.

Important: if you start a paid job or paid internship in the Netherlands, the rules change and you may need to switch to Dutch basic health insurance regardless of your nationality. Check this before you start working, not after.

Registering with a GP
In the Netherlands, a GP (huisarts) is your first point of contact for almost all non-emergency medical care - illness, prescriptions, referrals to specialists, mental health support. You cannot go directly to a specialist without a GP referral in most cases.

Register as soon as you arrive, not when you're ill.

For UT students, the go-to is Campus Huisarts UT, which is the GP practice on campus specifically for UT students. Register with them early in your first weeks.

When you register, bring your health insurance details and your BSN if you have it. If you haven't got your BSN yet, you can still register - just update your details once you do.

For urgent situations outside opening hours, you call the out-of-hours GP service (huisartsenpost), not the emergency number.

Emergencies
For life-threatening emergencies, call 112. For everything else that can't wait until your GP is open, contact the out-of-hours GP service. Going to A&E (spoedeisende hulp) without a referral is generally not how the Dutch system works for non-emergencies.

title: Phone and SIM card
summary: Get a Dutch or EU number for banking, DigiD, and everyday life in the Netherlands.
body:
A Dutch or reliable EU phone number is one of the first practical things to sort after arriving. You need it for DigiD two-factor authentication, opening a bank account, GP registration, and being reachable for housing and deliveries.

Choosing a provider
Popular options among students include Odido, KPN, and Lebara. Odido and KPN are the two main Dutch networks with good coverage across the country. Lebara is a budget option that runs on the KPN network and is widely used by international students.

Some providers offer physical SIM cards, others offer eSIMs - check which your phone supports before choosing. Most SIM cards can be picked up at supermarkets, electronics stores, or the provider's own shops.

Prepaid vs subscription
Prepaid SIMs are the easiest to set up - no contract, no BSN required, and you can top up as you go. They work well for the first few weeks.

If you plan to stay for a year or more, monthly or annual subscription plans are usually cheaper than prepaid. Most providers offer one-year or two-year plans with a fixed monthly data allowance. You may need a Dutch bank account and BSN to sign up for a contract, so it is worth sorting those first.

title: Transport and commuting
summary: Get around Enschede by bike or public transport.
body:
Enschede is a bike-friendly city and most students cycle daily. Or you can use the well-connected public transport system.

Getting a bike
The cheapest and most popular option is a second-hand bike. You can find them on Marktplaats (marktplaats.nl) or through student groups and associations. A decent second-hand bike typically costs €50-150.

Never buy a bike for €20 on the street - it's almost certainly stolen and you can be fined for riding it.

Beyond the bike itself, budget for a lock, lights, and rain gear. The Netherlands is wet and the weather turns fast. A rain jacket and waterproof trousers are not optional if you're cycling year-round.

Swapfiets is another popular option - a subscription bike service where you pay a monthly fee and they repair or replace the bike if something goes wrong. More expensive than buying second-hand but zero hassle.

Public transport
You can pay with a contactless bank card or phone directly on buses and trains. Alternatively, you can use an OV-chipkaart, which is a reloadable card for all public transport.

One important rule that catches people out: always check in and check out when using public transport. If you forget to check out, you get charged the maximum fare for that line.

For planning routes, install 9292 or NS on your phone. 9292 covers all public transport in the Netherlands (buses, trams, trains). NS is mostly used for trains. For getting around Enschede and to campus, buses are the main option if you're not cycling.

Enschede has a train station (Enschede Centraal) with connections to Amsterdam, Utrecht, and other major cities. There are several NS subscriptions you can explore depending on where and how often you plan to travel.

title: University life
summary: Know your academic platforms, campus navigation, and student support before your first week.
body:
Academic platforms
UT uses several platforms and you'll need all of them - they don't overlap. Canvas is where your course materials, assignments, and announcements live; check it daily, especially in your first week. Osiris handles grades, course registration, and your official academic record. MyUT is your personal student portal for timetables, events, and other academic information. TimeEdit is a web-based alternative to MyUT where your actual class schedule lives. All are accessible via your student login - if something isn't showing up, contact your study adviser straight away.

Finding your way around campus
The UT campus is large and a bit of a maze at first. MazeMap (available at mazemap.com or as an app) is an indoor campus map that most students use in their first few weeks. Download it before your first day of classes.

Eating on campus
There are cafeterias in several buildings serving hot meals, usually between €5–10. Many students also bring their own lunch from home.

Student associations and social life
Kick-In is UT's introduction week and the main way most international students meet people and find out about social life at UT. It happens right before the semester starts. ESN (Erasmus Student Network) organises events for international students throughout the year and has active WhatsApp groups. There are also nationality-based and interest-based student associations - for sports, culture, engineering, and more. The Student Union website has a full list.

Sports
UT has its own sports centre on campus (Sportcentrum) with gym access, group classes, and courts. Membership is available at a student rate - significantly cheaper than commercial gyms in the city.

Student support
These services are free and exist to be used. Your study adviser is your first point of contact for anything academic: course issues, falling behind, or changing programmes. The student buddy programme pairs you with a current student before you arrive - ask Student Services if you haven't been matched. The student psychologist and counsellor are available for free through UT; you don't need to be in crisis to use it.

Dutch language courses
UT offers Dutch language courses for international students, often heavily subsidised. Check the UT website and sign up early - spots fill up quickly.

title: Daily life
summary: Find food, explore Enschede, and settle into everyday student life.
body:
Groceries and markets
Enschede has a good mix of supermarkets. Albert Heijn and Jumbo have the widest product ranges. Lidl and Aldi are significantly cheaper for staples and worth the trip. An outdoor market runs on Tuesdays and Saturdays in the city centre with fresh produce, bread, and cheese. There is also a Spar inside the university campus - it is priced on the higher side, but it is the only supermarket on campus, so students tend to go there whenever they need essentials without making a separate trip into town.

Shopping and household basics
The city centre has most mainstream clothing and household chains. Action is good and affordably priced for household items, kitchen items, and small appliances. Primark near Enschede Centraal is good for cheap clothing. Kringloop (thrift) shops around the city sell second-hand furniture and kitchenware at very low prices - ideal for furnishing a room. Marktplaats (marktplaats.nl) is the Dutch equivalent of Craigslist and a great place to find second-hand furniture, bikes, and appliances locally.

Dining and eating out
The UT campus has student restaurants with affordable hot meals during term time. Enschede city centre has a wide range of international restaurants, falafel and kebab spots, Asian takeaways, and Dutch snack bars. The Orbit ITC guide below lists stores and dining options by category and is especially useful in your first weeks.
